coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot-embedded perovskite photodetectors with fast response and enhanced sensitivity through bulk defect passivation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Lee, David Sunghwan
Yun, Jeong-Seob
Heo, Jin Hyuck
Kim, Bong Woo
Im, Sang Hyuk
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesized by single phase solution chemistry are embedded in metal halide perovskite (MHP) films because they can act as passivating defects that are present throughout the MHP film, not only at the interface but also throughout the bulk of the film. The perovskite photodetectors composed of indium-doped tin oxide/SnO2/MHP with embedded GQDs/poly triarylamine/Au have the reduced dark current, increased detectivity, and fasted response speed due to lower trap density throughout the bulk MHP layer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Perovskites
Photodetectors
Fast response
Sensitivity
Bulk defect passivation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: D.S. Lee and J.-S. Yun contributed equally to this study. This study was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) under the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (Basic Science Research Program (No. 2021R1A5A6002853 ), and Nano-Material Technology Development Program (No. 2017M3A7B4041696 )) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy, Republic of Korea (New & Renewable Energy Core Technology Program of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) (No. 20183010013820 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a,b) TEM images of the GQD particles taken at (a) low magnification and (b) high magnification where the white dashed circles represent GQDs, (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum and photograph of the GQD dispersion (inset), and (d) PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths and photograph of the GQDs under UV light (inset). (For interpretation of the references to colour in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Photographs of the GQD dispersion solution compared to toluene, illuminated under no-light (top) and 365nm UV light (bottom). (b) UV–vis absorbance spectra, (c) XRD patterns and (d) magnification of the MAPbI3 (110) peak at ∼14.26°, (e) static-PL, and (f) TR PL measurements of the pristine and GQD-embedded MAPbI3 thin films. (g,h) Top-view (left) and cross-sectional (right) SEM images of the (g) pristine and (h) GQD-embedded MAPbI3 films. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Architecture of the fully fabricated photodetector device, (b) log(J)–V characteristics of the pristine and GQD-embedded MAPbI3 photodetector devices, (c) Comparison of response speeds to a single pulse of a green laser among the pristine MAPbI3 device (black), GQD-embedded device (cyan), and a silicon reference cell (red), (d, e) photocurrent responses of the (d) pristine and (e) GQD-embedded MAPbI3 devices to a pulsing green laser with increasing frequency from 1Hz to 1000Hz, and (f) log(J)–log(V) plots of the pristine and GQD-embedded MAPbI3 photodetector devices. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot-embedded perovskite photodetectors with fast response and enhanced sensitivity through bulk defect passivation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea University, 145 Anam-ro, Seongbuk-gu, Seoul 136-713, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea University, 145 Anam-ro, Seongbuk-gu, Seoul 136-713, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors have equally contributed to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesized by single phase solution chemistry are embedded in metal halide perovskite (MHP) films because they can act as passivating defects that are present throughout the MHP film, not only at the interface but also throughout the bulk of the film. The perovskite photodetectors composed of indium-doped tin oxide/SnO2/MHP with embedded GQDs/poly triarylamine/Au have the reduced dark current, increased detectivity, and fasted response speed due to lower trap density throughout the bulk MHP layer.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal halide perovskites (MHPs) are crystals of the ABX3 structure where the A-site is occupied by a monovalent cation, the B-site is occupied by a divalent metal ion, and the X-site is occupied by halogen anions. In the past decade, MHPs such as methylammonium lead triiodide (MAPbI3) have shown great promise as photodetector materials [1–4] due to their excellent optoelectronic properties such as direct bandgap, broad absorption spectrum in the UV to visible spectrum, small exciton binding energy and long charge carrier diffusion length [5–10]. In addition, MHPs show fairly strong defect tolerance compared to other materials due to their ionic nature, such as compensating for A-site and halogen vacancies [11,12]. However, further defects within the MHP crystals such as PbI2 or metallic Pb exposure may prove harmful as they could seriously degrade the optoelectronic performances of MHPs, so it is crucial to be able to control and minimize the number of defects throughout the MHP film for optoelectronic applications [11,13,14].

Photodetectors are devices that detect incident light and convert them into analyzable electric signals. Photodetectors are employed in a wide variety of applications including optical communication systems, video imaging, biomimetics and so on [15–17]. In order to fabricate high performing photodetectors, it is important to achieve high responsivity (R) to incident light, low noise, high specific detectivity (D*), and fast response speed [18]. In this retrospect, the good optoelectronic properties of MHPs are advantageous for efficiently converting light signals into electric signals. However, the presence of many trap states due to defects within MHP film can act as sources for undesired current leakage as well as deterrents against charge transportation, greatly inhibiting the photodetector performances [19,20].

Many studies have been performed in order to reduce the number of defects and subsequently suppress current leakage as well as facilitating efficient charge transfer/transport in perovskite photodetectors. For example, Dou et al. led one of the pioneering works for fabricating perovskite photodetectors in 2014, and emphasized the importance of choosing the proper materials at the interfaces [1]. They used bathocuproine (BCP) and poly[(9,9-bis(3′-(N,N-dimethylamino)propyl)-2,7-fluorene)-alt-2,7-(9,9-dioctylfluorene)] (PFN) as the contact buffer layers, and showed a remarkably low dark current density (Jd) of 1.5×10−11 mA/cm2 and high D* of 4×1014 Jones at 0V bias when using PFN, comparably better performing than silicon photodetectors. Dou et al. also noted that their perovskite photodetector device exhibited faster response speed (on the order of microseconds) than organic photodetectors (typically on the order of milliseconds), but still slower than commercial silicon photodetectors by a large margin (on the order of nanoseconds).

In 2016, Shen et al. demonstrated a fast response photodetector with response time reaching sub-nanosecond levels by covering the MHP surface with fullerene (C60) [21]. The complete coverage of the MAPbI3 film with C60 offered reduced leakage of current at the MAPbI3/C60/2,9-dimethyl-4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline (BCP) interface, allowing for a high D* of 7.8×1012 Jones at 700nm and a very fast device response time of 0.95ns through an aperture area of 0.04mm2. The authors accredited this impressive performance to the significant decrease in the density of defect states by nearly two orders, due to the passivation of MAPbI3 surface traps by C60, thus allowing fast electron extraction.

Recently, Li et al. also recognized the importance of passivating the defective surface of MHPs and discussed the choice of materials for passivation [2]. While one of the most commonly used materials for passivating the MHP interface is the fullerene derivative molecule, phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) [22,23], Li et al. pointed out the formation of new traps from using PCBM, and instead suggested the use of a nonfullerene-based material known as 2,2′-((2Z,2′Z)-((5,5′-(4,4,9,9,-tetrakis(4-hexylphenyl)-4,9-dihydro-sindaceno[1,2-b:5,6-b′]dithiophene-2,7-diyl)bis(4-((2-ethylhexyl)-oxy)thiophene-5,2-diyl))bis(methanylylidene))bis(3-oxo-2,3-dihydro-1H-indene-2,1-diylidene))dimalononitrile (IEICO). Their device exhibited a greatly improved response time of approximately 27ns as well as a wide linear dynamic range (LDR) of a little short of 200dB.

However, while there has been much discussion on the passivation of MHP and charge-transport layer interfaces, and much progress has been made, there has yet to be extensive discussion on the photodetector applications on the passivation of defects that exist within the bulk of the MHP films. In this paper, we synthesized and used graphene quantum dots (GQD) with organic ligands to deposit them within the bulk MHP film in an attempt to passivate the defects throughout the MHP film altogether. We argue that our method of GQD treatment on the MHP film not only affects the MHP surface, but also penetrates into the bulk material and passivates the grain boundary defects throughout, effectively creating a “GQD-embedded” perovskite layer. The photodetector with the GQD-embedded layer showed a decrease in dark current by one order, as well as a significant improvement in response speed.

d-(+)-glucose (G5767), hexadecylamine (90%, H7408), toluene (99.8%, 244511), lead(II) iodide (PbI2, 99%, 211168), bis(trifluoromethane)sulfonimide lithium salt (Li-TFSI, 449504), 4-tert-butylpyridine (t-BP, 96%, 142379), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8%, 227056), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9%, 472301) and acetonitrile (ACN, 99.8%, 271004) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Methylammonium iodide (MAI, 99.99%, SKU MS101000) was purchased from GreatCell Solar. Tin(IV) oxide (SnO2, 15% in H2O, MFCD00011244) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Poly(triaryl amine) (PTAA, MW =18,500g/mol) was purchased from EM-index. Acetic acid (glacial, UN2789) was purchased from Duksan. 1-hexanol (oil phase, 67063–0430) was purchased from Junsei Chemical Co., Ltd., and methanol (M0585) was purchased from Samchun. All materials were used as received without further purification.

For the synthesis of a GQD dispersion in toluene, first 1mL of 10wt.% glucose in DI water and 200μL of acetic acid were added per 10mL of 1-hexanol. The reactant mixture was heated at 80°C while being stirred at 1500rpm for 1h in a round bottom flask. 800mg of 1-hexadecylamine was added to the mixture. The mixture was then heated up to 150°C, being maintained for 1h with continuous 1500rpm stirring. During this step, the clear mixture turned dark brown, indicating the formation of GQDs. To terminate the reaction, the reaction mixture was quickly dispersed into 290mL of methanol. The mixture was sonicated in a water bath for 1h.

To purify the solution mixture, the GQDs suspended in methanol were stored in a freezer of below –15°C for over 12h and the dark brown precipitates were filtered through a PVDF filter (0.22μm pore size, hydrophilic PVDF, 47mm membrane/GVWP04700), and discarded. The supernatant was exchanged with toluene using a rotary evaporator and was precipitated in a freezer again. White flocculants were discarded via filtration and the resulting supernatants were further exchanged with solvents of choice.

For the fabrication of photodetector devices, the patterned indium-doped tin oxide (ITO) glasses were cleaned with detergent, ethanol, and acetone in a sonicator. Then, the substrates were treated with with Ar/O2 plasma. The SnO2, MHP, and PTAA layers were fabricated using a spin-coating process. The SnO2 precursor solution was prepared by mixing the SnO2 reagent in distilled water at a 1:5vol ratio. SnO2 was spin-coated on the ITO substrates at 500rpm for 3s, and subsequently 5000rpm for 40s, and then were heat-treated at 100°C for 20min. SnO2 was spin-coated on the substrates again using the same spin conditions, and then were annealed at 150°C for 1h. For the MHP precursor, a 1.4M MAPbI3 solution in 9:1vol ratio of DMF:DMSO was prepared by mixing MAI and PbI2 at a 1.01:1M ratio. The MHP precursor was spin-coated on the ITO/SnO2 substrates at 5000rpm for 17s. During this step, 300μL of toluene or the GQD dispersion was dripped on the spinning substrate 10s before the spinning stopped. The substrate was then placed on a hot plate of 150°C for 30s, and immediately placed on 100°C for 10min. Next, for the hole-transporting material (HTM), a 15mg(PTAA)/mL(toluene) solution containing 7.5μL of Li-TFSI/ACN (170mg/mL) and t-BP/ACN (1mL/mL) each per 1mL (HTM) was prepared. The HTM solution was spin-coated on the ITO/SnO2/MHP substrate at 3000rpm for 30s. Finally, an Au metal electrode of ∼50nm thickness was deposited on the substrates through thermal evaporation.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the GQDs were collected using a field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL). Raman spectroscopy was performed using a combined Raman FT-IR spectrometer (LabRam ARAMIS IR2, HORIBA JOBIN YVON), with a 532nm light-emitting diode. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was performed with a UV–vis spectrometer (3600 Plus, Shimadzu), and steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed with a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from an X-ray diffractometer (Model D, Rigaku) using a 0.156nm Cu Kα radiation source and a scan rate of 10°/min. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TR PL) decay measurements were taken using a photon counting spectrofluorometer (pc1, ISS) and a 373nm laser pulse with excitation energy of 747mW. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the MHP films were collected using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM), Quanta 250 FEG). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was performed using a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC4000, Perkin Elmer) by heating the sample from 0 to 150°C followed by cooling from 150 to 0°C with scan rate of 5°C/min, repeated for a total of two cycles. The photocurrent and dark current density curves were measured with a potentiostat (IviumStat, Ivium) under illumination of white light (100mW/cm2, AM 1.5G) from a solar simulator (PEC-L01, Peccell), which was calibrated with a Si-reference cell that was certified by Japanese Industrial Standards (JIS). The transient photoresponse was obtained using a sourcemeter (Keithley 2460, Keithley) and a pulsing 523nm light emitting diode (LED) source (ModuLight, Ivium). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at room temperature in dark conditions using a potentiostat (IviumStat, Ivium) with frequency range from 1MHz to 1Hz.

In order to embed GQD within the MAPbI3 thin film, we first synthesized a GQD dispersion in toluene, of which characterization is shown in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1(a) is a TEM image of the as-synthesized GQD. The magnified image in Fig. 1(b) shows multiple GQDs, which are outlined with a white dashed circle. Upon measuring the lattice fringe distance at ten different points and taking the average, we measured a lattice constant of approximately 0.241nm. This lattice constant, being a characteristic of GQDs, is in good agreement with multiple previous reports as well as theoretical calculations [24–28]. In order to further verify the synthesis of GQDs and their crystalline structure, we obtained Raman spectroscopy data of the GQD samples as shown in Fig. S1. The Raman spectroscopy result shows the GQD characteristic peaks as indicated by the D-band at 1358cm−1 and the G-band at 1579cm−1, which are in good agreement with previous reports regarding the characterization of GQDs [3,26–28]. Furthermore, the ID /IG ratio for the synthesized GQDs was calculated to be 0.771, indicating that the GQDs exhibit good crystallinity [29,30]. We measured the UV–vis absorption spectra and the static PL behavior of the synthesized GQD dispersion, shown in Fig. 1(c) and (d), respectively. The GQD dispersion, which is a dark-yellowish brown color in plain sight, exhibits the absorption shoulder at below 400nm. Under illumination by a 365nm UV source, the GQD dispersion emits a bright blue PL as shown in the inset of Fig. 1(d). After exciting the dispersion with light of multiple wavelengths spanning from 320nm to 430nm, we found that the 380nm excitation source brought forth the highest fluorescence emission at ∼461nm. The excitation dependence of the emission spectra is consistent with many previous reports, and is typically attributed to the variance in sizes and surface properties of the synthesized GQDs [3,31,32].

In order to deposit the perovskite films, we employed a one-step spin-coating process while dripping toluene for rapid crystallization when fabricating the pristine-MAPbI3 thin film, and used a GQD dispersion within toluene as the antisolvent when fabricating the GQD-embedded MAPbI3 thin film [33]. The GQD-dripped MAPbI3 film was scratched off the substrate, and the obtained powders were dispersed in toluene. TEM analysis of the perovskite film dispersion revealed that the GQDs are located on the MAPbI3 grain boundaries, as seen in Fig. S2 and evidenced by the different lattice constants of 0.214nm for GQDs and 0.312 for the tetragonal phase of MAPbI3 [34]. This confirms that the GQDs were embedded within the bulk MAPbI3 film. Fig. 2 (a) shows the photographic comparison between the two antisolvents and/or dispersion under illumination of no light (top) and UV light of 365nm (bottom). The GQD dispersion in toluene emits a bright blue fluorescence under UV excitation compared to toluene. The UV–vis absorption spectra for the pristine and GQD-embedded MAPbI3 films in Fig. 2(b) are nearly identical in intensity and shape. The XRD patterns in Fig. 2(c) did not show much difference in crystallographic patterns between both films, where the peaks at 14.3°, 20.2°, 23.6°, 24.6°, 28.6°, 32.0°, 35.2°, 40.8°, 42.8°, and 43.4° correspond to the (110), (200), (211), (202), (220), (222), (312), (400), (411), and (330) planes of the tetragonal MAPbI3 crystal, respectively [35]. The main peaks for both films are found at ∼14.3°, ∼28.6°, and ∼32.0°, indicating that the tetragonal phase for MAPbI3 is the dominating crystal structure despite GQD addition.

On the other hand, it is noteworthy that the peak for PbI2 at 12.8° is present for the pristine sample, but disappears for the GQD-embedded perovskite film [36]. This suggests that the addition of GQD plays a role in reducing the amount of defects that expose the PbI2 crystal structure within the MAPbI3 film, and thus may act as a passivation agent. Also, we can estimate the average crystal grain sizes of the pristine and GQD-embedded perovskite films by using the Scherrer equation:
(1)
t =
K λ
B cos
θ
where t is the average crystal grain size, K is a dimensionless shape factor of 0.9, λ is the incident X-ray wavelength of 0.154nm, B is the full width at half maximum (FWHM), and θ is the diffraction angle [37,38]. By applying this to the main peak for both films, shown in Fig. 2(d), we calculated average grain sizes of ∼85.5nm and ∼94.6nm for the pristine and GQD-embedded MAPbI3 films, respectively. Accordingly, the addition of GQDs slightly increases the crystal grain size.

Next, we measured the PL characteristics of the pristine and GQD-embedded films. The steady-state PL spectra shown in Fig. 2(e) illustrate that the PL intensity sharply increases with the addition of GQD. Also, the TRPL decay curves in Fig. 2(f) illustrate an increase in PL decay lifetime for the GQD-embedded MAPbI3 films. Similar PL behavior have been often reported to be observed when trap states within the perovskite film are passivated, which may be explained by the diminishing PbI2 peak in the XRD patterns in Fig. 2(c) [39,40]. We also took the top-view and cross-sectional view SEM images of the pristine and GQD-embedded MAPbI3 films, which are shown in Fig. 2(g) and (h), respectively. The grain sizes of the GQD-embedded film appear to be slightly larger on average than those of the pristine film, showing good agreement with the calculation from Scherrer’s equation in Fig. 2(d). Both films exhibit single crystal growth of similar thickness, as seen in the cross-sectional view SEM images. In order to confirm that the growth in grain size was caused by the embedded GQDs rather than simply the presence of GQDs, we also prepared MAPbI3 films that were fully annealed and subsequently treated with spin coating of GQD dispersion. Comparison between the top-view SEM images of the GQD-embedded film, labeled as “GQD”, and the GQD-spin coating film, labeled as “GQD-spin”, in Fig. S3 revealed that grain growth did not occur as much for the GQD-spin MAPbI3 film. This confirms that the embedded GQDs were responsible for grain growth of the MAPbI3 compared to the pristine perovskite film.

These combined results seem to suggest that the role of the GQD dispersion is strongly related to the passivation of defects within the MAPbI3 film, such as passivating A-site defects that would otherwise lead to an excessive exposure of PbI2 crystals [41]. The GQDs used in this work have been surface-treated with hexadecylamine during the synthesis process, and thus possess an ample source of carbon as well as alkyl amines that could possibly react with and passivate the existing defects, despite its insulating properties as a long alkyl chain molecule. Hexadecylamine is a sixteen-chain-alkyl amine, which has a melting point of approximately 46.8°C [42]. The DSC plot for solid phase hexadecylamine in Fig. S4 shows its phase change, presumably melting into liquid phase, at approximately 45.4°C during the heating process and another phase change, back to solid phase, at 31.8°C during the cooling process. During the annealing process of the as-fabricated perovskite film, the substrate and the film are heated up to 150°C for approximately 30s, and then are left at 100°C for at least 10min before additional processing. We believe that it is during the perovskite annealing process when the hexadecylamine becomes a liquid-like phase and is able to heal the perovskite grains and bulk defects because the hexadecylamine can dissolve the MAPbI3 perovskite and reduce the formation of PbI2 defects from the MAPbI3. We also prepared GQD samples from the synthesized GQD dispersion by fully evaporating the solvent at above 100°C in a convection oven, until no more mass loss due to evaporation was observed. When cooled to room temperature, no fluidity of the sample could be seen. However, when placed on a hot plate set at 100°C, which is the annealing temperature for the MAPbI3 film, we could visually observe a viscous flow of our sample by tilting the sample vial, as shown in Fig. S5. By tilting the vial to the opposite side, the sample would slowly flow towards the bottom of the tilted vial. When removed from the hot plate and left to cool, the sample would no longer show fluidity again. This process was repeatable for at least ten times. This proves that the GQDs with hexadecylamine ligands become a liquid-like phase during the MAPbI3 annealing process, thus are capable of slightly dissolving the perovskite and reducing defects via recrystallization at the grain boundary.

In order to determine the device effects of GQD-embedding on MAPbI3 perovskites, we then fabricated full photodetector devices to compare device performances of pristine and GQD-embedded MAPbI3. The overall device architecture is illustrated in Fig. 3 (a). Fig. 3(b) shows the current density (J)–voltage (V) curves of the fully fabricated photodetector devices. While there is barely any difference in the photocurrent values for both devices, the photocurrent of the GQD-embedded device is slightly lower, which we attribute the slight increase in insulating properties due to the introduction of long chain alkyl amine ligands attached to the GQDs. However, the dark current density of the GQD-embedded device appears to be approximately one order lower than that of the pristine device throughout the photoconductive region at negative bias. At a bias of Vbias
=–0.5V, the dark current densities for the pristine and GQD-embedded devices are 6.32×10−5 A/cm2 and 7.75×10−6 A/cm2, respectively. This results in specific detectivities of 3.62×1010 Jones and 8.27×1010 Jones for the respective devices, calculated using the following equation for specific detectivity:
(2)
D *
=
J
p h
/
L
i n
2 q
J d
where Jph is the photocurrent density, Lin is the incident light intensity of 0.1W/cm2, q is the elementary charge of 1.602×10−19 C, and Jd is the dark current density, assuming that the dark current is dominated by shot noise as opposed to thermal and flicker noise [1,18]. The reduced dark current of the GQD-embedded device can be attributed to the decrease in trap density, due to trap state passivation offered by the GQDs at the MAPbI3 grain boundaries [19].

Next, in order to determine concentration effects, we prepared various concentrations of the GQD dispersion by diluting the solution by 50, 25 and 10 folds with toluene, labeled as “GQD 0.02x”, “GQD 0.04x”, and “GQD 0.1x”, respectively. We used the diluted GQD dispersions to create the GQD-embedded MAPbI3 photodetector devices. The best performing devices at –0.5V bias for the pristine MAPbI3, GQD 0.02x, GQD 0.04x, and GQD 0.1x conditions exhibited dark current density values of 5.33×10−6, 8.93×10−6, 1.23×10−6, and 3.35×10−6 A/cm2, and specific detectivity values of 1.09×1011, 1.10×1011, 2.79×1011, and 1.57×1011 Jones, respectively. On average, the dark current decreased with the addition of GQDs up to 4% dilution, and increased with more GQD embedding. Similarly, the detectivity increased up to 4% dilution, and decreased afterwards. The comparison of dark current densities as well as box plots of the varying GQD concentrations samples are shown in Fig. S6. While the introduction of embedded GQDs within the bulk MAPbI3 film decrease dark current and increase detectivity up to a certain extent, we can also see that excessive embedding of the GQDs will begin to degrade device performance, for which we offer the two following explanations. First of all, since the GQD dispersion is accompanied by long-chain alkyl amine ligands attached to the GQDs, then an excessive amount of GQDs will also introduce a large amount of the ligands that have an insulating property. The excessive amount of insulating carbon chains within the bulk MAPbI3 film will decrease the overall conductivity of the film, thereby reducing the overall responsivity. Second, when an excessive amount of GQDs is introduced into the perovskite film, the GQDs may also act as nucleation centers during the crystallization process of the perovskite film [43]. This would then lead to a perovskite film with very small grain size and abundant pinholes as seen in the top-view SEM image in Fig. S7. Such a perovskite film is undesirable for photodetector applications since the large amount of trap sites will increase the overall noise of the device, hence degrading performance such as detectivity. Therefore, an adequate amount of GQDs must be embedded into the MAPbI3 film in order to bring out optimum performance.

We also compared the response times of the two devices using a green LED pulse and a sourcemeter to measure the responsive photocurrent. Fig. 3(c) portrays the response of both devices to a single pulse at 2Hz, and the response of a silicon reference cell is included for comparison. Defining the rise and decay times as the time it takes for a device to reach 90% and 10% of the maximum current values, respectively, the rise times of the pristine and GQD-embedded MAPbI3 devices, and the Si reference cell were ∼282ms, ∼220ms, and ∼221ms, respectively, while the decay times were ∼163ms, ∼159ms, and ∼158ms for the respective devices. First, it is worth noticing that the response times of the Si reference cell is abnormally high compared to many other reports that claim the response time of crystalline silicon to lie within at least the microsecond scale [44,45]. This is most likely due to the photoresponse signal being gated by rise and decay times of the LED pulse. Therefore, the measured response time of the silicon reference cell would actually represent a combination of the Si-only response as well as the LED pulse delay. Nonetheless, it should also be noted that, while the pristine MAPbI3 device cannot follow the speed of the Si reference cell, lagging behind by approximately 61ms and 5ms for rise and decay times, respectively, the response time of the GQD-embedded device nearly coincides with that of the Si reference cell. This proves that the addition of GQD has significantly improved the response time of the MAPbI3 device.

In order to better visualize the difference in response speed of the two devices, we modulated the incident light pulse frequency from 1Hz to 1000Hz and observed the photocurrent response behaviors, shown in Fig. 3(d) for pristine MAPbI3 device and Fig. 3(e) for the GQD-embedded device. In Fig. 3(d), the maximum photocurrent value of the pristine device begins to decrease not far into the frequency modulation, whereas the GQD-embedded device in Fig. 3(e) maintains its maximum photocurrent value further into the frequency modulation. This shows that the addition of GQD may be capable of increasing the response speed of the MAPbI3 photodetectors by at least one or two orders. This enhancement may also be a result of less defects present within the perovskite film, since the charge trapping and de-trapping process caused by the presence of traps would slow down the charge transport speed through the perovskite layer [20,21].

Finally, in order to verify that the number of traps did actually decrease as a result of GQD-embedding, we calculated the trap densities in each device by finding the trap filled limit voltage (VTFL) from a log–log plot of both Jd–V curves, presented in Fig. 3(f). Assuming that the thickness of the active layer would not be much different from the perovskite layer thickness shown in Fig. 2(g) and (h), we used the following equation to calculate trap densities:
(3)
V
T F L
=
e
N t
d 2
2 ε
ε 0
where VTFL is the trap filled limit voltage, e is the elementary charge of an electron, Nt is number of the overall trap densities, d is the active layer thickness, ε is the dielectric constant of MAPbI3 (∼28.8), and ε0 is the vacuum permittivity of 8.854×10−14 F/m [13,46,47]. Since the thicknesses of both films are almost the same, we estimate approximately 360nm for the perovskite layer thicknesses. Using the VTFL values of 1.02 and 0.72 for pristine and GQD-embedded MAPbI3 devices, we calculated the overall trap densities to be 2.51×1016 cm−3 and 1.77×1016 cm−3, respectively. The reduced amount of trap sites in the GQD-embedded film by approximately a factor of two, compared to the pristine MAPbI3 film, would be responsible for less noise and recombination, thus faster response speed. To further verify this behavior, we performed EIS on the pristine and GQD-embedded MAPbI3 devices, as shown in Fig. S8. At a quick glance, we can see that the GQD-embedded device exhibits higher recombination resistance, denoted by R2 in the fitting parameters. The GQD-embedded device also shows lower capacitance in the high frequency regime, typically referred to as the geometrical capacitance [48,49]. This is in good agreement with our results above where the embedded GQDs decreased trap density, hence reducing the amount of recombination sites within the bulk MAPbI3 film, and allowed for a faster response speed due to lower device capacitance. On the other hand, we can see that the series resistance slightly increases for the GQD-embedded device compared to the pristine device. This is expected as we had mentioned above that the long-chain ligands attached to the GQDs possess an insulating property. However, this effect does not significantly degrade the photodetector performance of our device, since we can see that the difference between series resistances of the two devices are very small. These results further corroborate our hypothesis that the GQDs embedded within the MAPbI3 film act as defect passivation agents that induces more efficient charge transport within the bulk of the film, thus effectively reducing trap-induced dark current, increasing device detectivity, and enhancing the device response speed.

In this work, we have fabricated and compared GQD-embedded MAPbI3 photodetector devices by using a toluene dispersion of small size GQDs of five to six nanometers that we synthesized, as the antisolvent for a one-step dripping process during the deposition of the perovskite film in order to effectively embed GQDs throughout the MAPbI3 film. This process allows the GQDs to act as a delivery medium for the long chain alkyl amine ligands, attached to its surface, throughout the bulk perovskite film. The long carbon chains become liquified during the annealing process of the perovskite film, thus more effectively distributing itself as a fluid through the bulk and act as perovskite defect passivation agents, effectively healing and reducing the number of trap states within the film. The GQD-embedded photodetector device that has undergone this passivation process then proceeds to exhibit a reduced dark current density and thus an increased specific detectivity compared to the pristine MAPbI3 device, and also shows an enhanced response speed. However, there remains a tradeoff between the advantages of noise reduction and response speed enhancement, and the disadvantages of photoresponsivity loss due to the insulating property of the GQD ligands. Therefore, further optimization and quantification may be possible by altering variables such as the concentration of the GQD dispersion and the types of ligands attached to the GQDs. We believe that our work may open further discussion to passivating not only the MHP interfaces, but also the bulk material itself in order to bring perovskite photodetectors closer to the competition against other commercial devices.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: D.S. Lee and J.-S. Yun contributed equally to this study.
